attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man presents to his General Practice clinic with a three-month history of fatigue, abdominal pain, and difficulty concentrating at work. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He works as a house painter, specialising in renovating older homes. On examination, he appears pale. His vital signs are within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals mild, diffuse tenderness without guarding. Blood tests show

  • haemoglobin 105 g/L (130-170)

  • mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 78 fL (80-100)

  • white cell count 7.5 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)

  • platelets 220 x 10^9/L (150-400)

  • blood film: basophilic stippling of red blood cells
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step of investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms and laboratory findings suggestive of lead poisoning. The combination of fatigue, abdominal pain, and cognitive difficulties, along with a microcytic anaemia and basophilic stippling on blood film, are characteristic features. His occupation as a painter renovating older homes is a significant risk factor, as old paint often contains lead.

The most appropriate next step of investigation is to measure the patient's lead level. This test will confirm the diagnosis of lead poisoning and guide treatment decisions. It's essential to establish the diagnosis before initiating any specific therapy.

A zinc level would not be appropriate as the first investigation in this case. While zinc deficiency can cause various symptoms, it does not typically cause the specific haematological findings seen here, particularly the basophilic stippling.

Haemoglobin electrophoresis is not indicated as the first step in this case. While it can help diagnose haemoglobinopathies that might cause microcytic anaemia, the presence of basophilic stippling and the occupational risk factors make lead poisoning a more likely diagnosis.

A pyridoxine level (vitamin B6) is not the most appropriate next step. While pyridoxine deficiency can cause anaemia, it typically presents with a sideroblastic anaemia without basophilic stippling as seen in this case. Moreover, it doesn't explain the other symptoms or the occupational risk factor.

A copper level would not be the most appropriate next step of investigation. While copper deficiency can cause anaemia, it typically presents with a normocytic or macrocytic picture. Microcytosis with ringed sideroblasts may also be seen, but not basophilic stippling as described in this case.

If lead poisoning is confirmed, management typically involves removing the source of exposure and, in cases of significant toxicity, chelation therapy. The patient's workplace would need to be assessed, and he may require temporary removal from his current job duties.
learningPoints: Lead poisoning can cause a microcytic anaemia with basophilic stippling of red blood cells.
Occupational history is crucial in diagnosing lead poisoning.
Chelation therapy is the primary treatment for significant lead poisoning.
references: Frith D, Yeung K, Thrush S, Hunt BJ, Hubbard JG. Lead poisoning--a differential diagnosis for abdominal pain. Lancet. 2005 Dec 17;366(9503):2146. doi: 10.1016/S0140-6736(05)67893-2. PMID: 16360796.
Markowitz M. Lead Poisoning: An Update. Pediatr Rev. 2021 Jun;42(6):302-315. doi: 10.1542/pir.2020-0026. PMID: 34074717.
